title: Two halves of a marriage
summary: A couple, married thirty-four years, sit at the kitchen table and discuss whether the parts of themselves they kept secret were betrayals.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Did you ever read my journal?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: No. Did you ever read mine?

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: No. I thought about it twice. Both times I put it back.

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Once in 1998 and once in 2011?

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: How did you guess that?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Because those are the years I would have been most worth reading.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Was there something I should have known?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Things I never told you. None of them another person.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: I assumed that.

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: I had a year, in 1998, when I thought about leaving you. I told no one. I wrote it down.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: What stopped you?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Several small things. The way you stood at the window in February when you thought I was asleep. The fact that I had nowhere I wanted to go. The cost.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: It sounds honest. Was 2011 the same?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Worse, in some ways. 2011 was the year I realised I had a kind of professional ambition I had been hiding from you, because I did not think you would respect it.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: What was the ambition?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: To write a novel. I wrote three chapters that year and threw them out. I told you I had been doing tax forms.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Why did you think I would not respect it?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Because your parents thought writers were lazy. I was afraid of what you would say if I failed. You can fail at writing for years.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Minh, my parents are wrong about most things.

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: I know. But that was the room I grew up arguing with. I confused you with the room.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Were the chapters good?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: I don't know. I have not read them. I kept the third one. Top shelf of the cupboard above the rice.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: May I ask one thing?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Do you think the parts of yourself you kept secret were a betrayal of me?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: I have asked myself that for years. I have a careful answer.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Tell me.

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: If I had hidden a person, yes. If I had hidden money we shared, yes. If I had hidden my opinion of you in a way that changed how I treated you, also yes. But what I hid were doubts I was working through alone, and an ambition I was too afraid to test in front of you. Those are different from betrayals. They are more like rooms I built inside the house where I lived with you, that I did not show you the key to.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Some of those rooms I might have liked to sit in.

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: I know. That is what I am sorry for. Not for having the rooms.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Minh, I have rooms too.

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: I know that. I have never asked.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Did you want to ask?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: Sometimes. I decided the rooms were yours to open.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: That is more generous than what I did. I never wanted to know. I was afraid the rooms were people.

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: They weren't.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: I believe you. Minh — would you read the third chapter to me tonight?

body.speaker: Minh
body.text: It might be terrible.

body.speaker: Hai
body.text: Then it might be terrible. But you should read it.

grammarNotes.title: Third conditional + counterfactual reasoning
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để bàn về 'điều đáng lẽ có thể xảy ra' nhưng không xảy ra, B2 dùng câu điều kiện loại 3: 'If + had + V3, would have + V3'. Trong bài: 'If I had hidden a person, yes.' / 'If I had hidden money we shared, yes.' Minh dùng nhiều câu điều kiện loại 3 song song để cẩn thận phân biệt các kịch bản giả định: cái nào sẽ là phản bội, cái nào thì không.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B2 speakers use the third conditional ('If + past perfect, would have + past participle') to reason about what didn't happen. Minh stacks parallel third conditionals to draw careful distinctions: 'If I had hidden a person, yes. If I had hidden money we shared, yes. If I had hidden my opinion of you in a way that changed how I treated you, also yes.' The structure lets him isolate exactly when secrecy becomes betrayal.

translationVi:
Hải: Em có bao giờ đọc nhật ký của anh không?
Minh: Không. Anh có bao giờ đọc nhật ký của em không?
Hải: Không. Anh đã định hai lần. Cả hai lần anh đều bỏ lại.
Minh: Một lần năm 1998 và một lần năm 2011?
Hải: Sao em đoán được vậy?
Minh: Vì đó là những năm em đáng-đọc-nhất.
Hải: Có gì anh đáng lẽ phải biết không?
Minh: Những chuyện em chưa bao giờ nói với anh. Không có chuyện nào là một người khác.
Hải: Anh đã đoán vậy.
Minh: Em từng có một năm, năm 1998, em nghĩ đến chuyện bỏ anh. Em không kể với ai. Em viết xuống giấy.
Hải: Cái gì đã ngăn em?
Minh: Vài chuyện nhỏ. Cách anh đứng bên cửa sổ tháng Hai khi anh tưởng em ngủ. Sự thật là em không có nơi nào em muốn đến. Chi phí.
Hải: Nghe thành thật. 2011 cũng vậy à?
Minh: Tệ hơn, theo một nghĩa nào đó. 2011 là năm em nhận ra em có một loại tham vọng nghề nghiệp mà em đã giấu anh, vì em không nghĩ anh sẽ tôn trọng nó.
Hải: Tham vọng đó là gì?
Minh: Viết một cuốn tiểu thuyết. Em viết ba chương năm đó rồi vứt đi. Em bảo anh là em đang làm tờ khai thuế.
Hải: Sao em nghĩ anh sẽ không tôn trọng nó?
Minh: Vì bố mẹ anh cho rằng người viết là lười. Em sợ anh sẽ nói gì nếu em thất bại. Người ta có thể thất bại trong việc viết hàng năm trời.
Hải: Minh, bố mẹ anh sai về đa số mọi thứ.
Minh: Em biết. Nhưng đó là căn phòng em đã lớn lên trong khi tranh cãi với nó. Em đã nhầm anh với căn phòng đó.
Hải: Mấy chương đó có hay không?
Minh: Em không biết. Em chưa đọc lại. Em giữ chương thứ ba. Ngăn trên cùng tủ phía trên thùng gạo.
Hải: Anh hỏi một câu được không?
Minh: Anh cứ hỏi.
Hải: Em có nghĩ những phần của bản thân mà em giữ kín là một sự phản bội với anh không?
Minh: Em đã tự hỏi mình câu đó nhiều năm. Em có một câu trả lời cẩn trọng.
Hải: Em nói đi.
Minh: Nếu em giấu một người khác, có. Nếu em giấu tiền của hai vợ chồng, có. Nếu em giấu cách em nghĩ về anh theo kiểu làm thay đổi cách em đối xử với anh, cũng có. Nhưng cái em giấu là những hoài nghi em tự xử lý một mình, và một tham vọng em quá sợ để thử trước mặt anh. Những thứ đó khác phản bội. Chúng giống những căn phòng em đã xây bên trong ngôi nhà em sống với anh, mà em không đưa anh chìa khoá.
Hải: Có những căn phòng đó anh có thể đã thích ngồi trong.
Minh: Em biết. Đó là điều em xin lỗi. Không phải vì đã có những căn phòng.
Hải: Minh, anh cũng có những căn phòng.
Minh: Em biết. Em chưa bao giờ hỏi.
Hải: Em có muốn hỏi không?
Minh: Có khi. Em quyết định những căn phòng đó là của anh để anh mở.
Hải: Như vậy còn rộng lượng hơn cái anh đã làm. Anh chưa bao giờ muốn biết. Anh đã sợ những căn phòng đó là người.
Minh: Không phải đâu.
Hải: Em tin em. Minh — tối nay em đọc chương thứ ba cho anh nghe được không?
Minh: Có thể nó tệ lắm.
Hải: Vậy thì có thể nó tệ. Nhưng em nên đọc nó.

questions.prompt: Did either of them ever read the other's journal?
options: No — both say they did not.
Both did, but never admitted it.
questions.explanation: Hai: 'I thought about it twice. Both times I put it back.' Minh: 'No.'

questions.prompt: How does Minh correctly guess the two years Hai was tempted to read?
options: Because those are the years Minh would have been 'most worth reading.'
Because Hai once mentioned the dates.
Because Hai always reads on anniversaries.
questions.explanation: 'Because those are the years I would have been most worth reading.'

questions.prompt: What had Minh thought about doing in 1998?
questions.explanation: 'I had a year, in 1998, when I thought about leaving you.'
questions.hint: Minh's reply when Hai asks if there was something she should have known.

options: Several small things: the way Hai stood at the window, the fact that Minh had nowhere she wanted to go, the cost.
questions.explanation: Minh lists all three reasons in her reply about what stopped her.

questions.prompt: What had Minh hidden from Hai in 2011?
options: A professional ambition: to write a novel.
Plans to move to a different city.
questions.explanation: 'I realised I had a kind of professional ambition I had been hiding from you… To write a novel.'

questions.prompt: Why did Minh hide the writing ambition?
options: Because Hai's parents thought writers were lazy, and Minh was afraid of being judged if she failed.
Because Hai had also wanted to write.
Because Minh did not have time.
questions.explanation: 'Because your parents thought writers were lazy. I was afraid of what you would say if I failed.'

questions.prompt: What does Minh mean by saying 'I confused you with the room'?
options: Minh attributed her in-laws' beliefs to Hai personally, even though they may not be his.
Minh thought she was talking to a wall.
Minh thought their apartment was haunted.
Minh forgot which room Hai was in.
questions.explanation: Hai says 'my parents are wrong about most things,' and Minh replies that the parents' room was the one she grew up arguing with — she conflated Hai with that room.
questions.hint: Look at the exchange about Hai's parents.

questions.prompt: Where did Minh keep the third chapter of the novel?
options: Top shelf of the cupboard above the rice.
questions.explanation: 'Top shelf of the cupboard above the rice.'

questions.prompt: How does Minh distinguish her secrets from betrayals?
options: Hiding a person, shared money, or an opinion that changes treatment would have been betrayals; doubts worked through alone and an untested ambition are not.
Anything written in a journal is automatically not a betrayal.
Time-limited secrets are betrayals; permanent secrets are not.
Only physical secrets count as betrayals.
questions.explanation: Minh's three parallel 'If I had hidden…' sentences and the closing distinction.
questions.hint: Look at Minh's three 'If I had hidden…' sentences.

questions.prompt: What metaphor does Minh use for the things she kept secret?
options: Rooms she built inside the house where she lived with Hai, that she did not show him the key to.
Boxes she hid in the attic.
questions.explanation: 'They are more like rooms I built inside the house where I lived with you, that I did not show you the key to.'

questions.prompt: What is Minh sorry for, specifically?
options: Not for having the rooms, but for not showing Hai the key to some of them.
questions.explanation: 'That is what I am sorry for. Not for having the rooms.'
questions.hint: Look at the line just after Hai says he might have liked to sit in the rooms.

questions.prompt: How does Hai describe his own behaviour with Minh's secrets?
options: He never wanted to know because he was afraid the rooms were people.
He read her journal twice and hid it.
He kept a list of his suspicions.
questions.explanation: 'I never wanted to know. I think I was afraid the rooms were people.'

questions.prompt: What does Hai ask Minh to do at the very end?
options: Read the third chapter of the novel to him tonight.
Show him the rest of the journal.
questions.explanation: 'Would you read the third chapter to me tonight?'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That long marriages require total transparency.
That secrecy and betrayal are different categories.
That some private rooms are better kept private.
That afterwards is not too late to share something.
questions.explanation: Minh explicitly distinguishes private 'rooms' from betrayals, and Hai says 'the rooms were yours to open' — neither argues for total transparency.
questions.hint: Look at what Hai says about Minh's privacy.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best captures Minh's careful answer about secrecy?
options: 'Doubts I was working through alone, and an ambition I was too afraid to test in front of you… are different from betrayals.'
questions.explanation: This is the key clause of Minh's structured answer.
questions.hint: Minh's longest reply, where she distinguishes types of secrecy.

But what I hid were doubts I was working through alone, and an ambition I was too afraid to test in front of you. Those are different from betrayals. They are more like {{b12}} I built inside the house where I lived with you, that I did not show you the key to.

blanks.explanation: Those were the years Minh was 'most worth reading.'
None of the secrets were another person.
The cost was one of the reasons she did not leave.
A professional ambition she had been hiding.
She wanted to write a novel.
His parents thought writers were lazy.
She confused him with the room.
Hai asks whether the secrets were a betrayal.
Rooms inside the house she lived in with him.
Not sorry for having the rooms, only for not showing the key.
The rooms were his to open, not hers to walk into.
He was afraid the rooms were people.
Minh warns it might be terrible.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Minh distinguishes 'rooms I built inside the house where I lived with you' from betrayals. Is there a private room you keep inside a close relationship — a long-held doubt, an untested ambition? Could you describe to the other person what is in the room, without yet showing them the key?
